comments.text: I think there may still be a typo in the definition of $F(n,\alpha)$: should "largest $k$" perhaps be "smallest $k$"? As currently written, for any $k>n$ there are no induced subgraphs of $K_n$ on at least $k$ vertices, so the stated property holds vacuously. Hence every $k>n$ is admissible, and the set of admissible $k$'s has no largest element. Even if one implicitly restricts $k\le n$, the word "largest" still seems inconsistent with the stated logarithmic behaviour: for fixed $0\le \alpha<1/2$, a nearly balanced colouring of $K_n$ makes $k=n$ admissible for all sufficiently large $n$, so the largest admissible $k\le n$ would be $n$, not $\Theta(\log n)$. Also, the endpoint $\alpha=1/2$ seems incompatible with the strict "more than" condition, since the two colour counts in $H$ sum to $\binom{|H|}{2}$. So perhaps the intended formulation is with "smallest $k$" and $0\le \alpha<1/2$?
Should "in any $2$-coloring" be "there exists some $2$-coloring"? (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
Judging by the original source [Er90b, p. 21], your interpretation of the problem seems to be correct.
And "subgraph H" should probably be "induced subgraph H" (of Kn).
